exercises.title: Three cases for linear search
exercises.instructions:
Fill the complexity table for linear search `int[] arr, int target`:

| Case    | When it occurs                  | Complexity |
|---------|--------------------------------|------------|
| Best    | ?                              | ?          |
| Average | ?                              | ?          |
| Worst   | ?                              | ?          |
exercises.expected_solution:
| Case    | When it occurs                          | Complexity |
|---------|----------------------------------------|------------|
| Best    | target is at index 0 (first element)    | O(1)       |
| Average | target at random position (avg = n/2)   | O(n)       |
| Worst   | target is last OR not in array at all   | O(n)       |

Notes:
  Best case O(1): We check arr[0] first — instant hit. Lucky!
  Average O(n/2) = O(n): Drop constant 1/2 → O(n).
  Worst O(n): Must scan all n elements.

Average == Worst for linear search (both O(n)) because:
  Even O(n/2) with a constant 1/2 dropped is still O(n).

Interview: 'Linear search is O(n) time in the worst and average case,
  O(1) in the best case (target at index 0).'

test_cases.expected:
| Case    | When it occurs                          | Complexity |
|---------|----------------------------------------|------------|
| Best    | target is at index 0 (first element)    | O(1)       |
| Average | target at random position (avg = n/2)   | O(n)       |
| Worst   | target is last OR not in array at all   | O(n)       |

Notes:
  Best case O(1): We check arr[0] first — instant hit. Lucky!
  Average O(n/2) = O(n): Drop constant 1/2 → O(n).
  Worst O(n): Must scan all n elements.

Average == Worst for linear search (both O(n)) because:
  Even O(n/2) with a constant 1/2 dropped is still O(n).

Interview: 'Linear search is O(n) time in the worst and average case,
  O(1) in the best case (target at index 0).'

hints: Best case: found immediately at first index
Worst case: not found, scan all n
Average: drop constant 1/2 → same as worst

exercises.title: Three cases for quicksort
exercises.instructions:
Fill the complexity table for quicksort:

| Case    | Input shape that triggers it    | Complexity |
|---------|--------------------------------|------------|
| Best    | ?                              | ?          |
| Average | ?                              | ?          |
| Worst   | ?                              | ?          |

Also: what is the fix for the worst case?
exercises.expected_solution:
| Case    | Input shape that triggers it              | Complexity |
|---------|------------------------------------------|------------|
| Best    | Pivot always splits array in equal halves | O(n log n) |
| Average | Random input, roughly balanced splits     | O(n log n) |
| Worst   | Sorted/reverse-sorted + last-elem pivot   | O(n²)      |

Worst case in detail:
  Pivot = last element, array already sorted: [1,2,3,4,5]
  Pivot=5: left partition has 4 elements, right has 0
  Pivot=4: left partition has 3 elements, right has 0
  ...n partitions each of size n-1, n-2, ... → O(n²)

This is NOT theoretical — sorted input is COMMON in production
  (sorted product IDs, sorted timestamps, already-sorted user IDs)

Fix options:
  1. Random pivot: pick random index as pivot → random input every time
  2. Median-of-three: pick median of first, middle, last elements
  3. Three-way partition: Dutch National Flag for many duplicates
  4. Shuffle input first (Fisher-Yates) then quicksort

Java Arrays.sort(): uses dual-pivot quicksort with safeguards
  Falls back to heap sort when recursion depth too large

test_cases.expected:
| Case    | Input shape that triggers it              | Complexity |
|---------|------------------------------------------|------------|
| Best    | Pivot always splits array in equal halves | O(n log n) |
| Average | Random input, roughly balanced splits     | O(n log n) |
| Worst   | Sorted/reverse-sorted + last-elem pivot   | O(n²)      |

Worst case in detail:
  Pivot = last element, array already sorted: [1,2,3,4,5]
  Pivot=5: left partition has 4 elements, right has 0
  Pivot=4: left partition has 3 elements, right has 0
  ...n partitions each of size n-1, n-2, ... → O(n²)

This is NOT theoretical — sorted input is COMMON in production
  (sorted product IDs, sorted timestamps, already-sorted user IDs)

Fix options:
  1. Random pivot: pick random index as pivot → random input every time
  2. Median-of-three: pick median of first, middle, last elements
  3. Three-way partition: Dutch National Flag for many duplicates
  4. Shuffle input first (Fisher-Yates) then quicksort

Java Arrays.sort(): uses dual-pivot quicksort with safeguards
  Falls back to heap sort when recursion depth too large

hints: Worst case: sorted input + last-element pivot → O(n²)
Best/average: O(n log n) — same as merge sort
Fix: random pivot removes the sorted-input vulnerability

exercises.title: Demonstrate amortized ArrayList.add()
exercises.instructions:
Show that ArrayList.add() is amortized O(1) by tracking resize events:
1. Simulate a dynamic array that doubles on resize
2. Track: total elements added, total resize cost, amortized cost per add
3. Print a table for n=1,2,4,8,16,32 showing resize moments
4. Verify: total_resize_cost / n ≤ 2.0 (amortized ≤ O(1) × 2)
hints: Resize at capacity boundary: copy all existing elements (O(n))
Doublings: 1,2,4,8,16... — geometric series sums to 2n
Amortized = totalCost/n ≤ 2.0 → O(1)

exercises.title: Trigger quicksort worst case deliberately
exercises.instructions:
Show that naive quicksort (last-element pivot) degrades to O(n²) on sorted input:
1. Implement quicksort with last-element pivot + comparison counter
2. Count comparisons for random array (should be ~n log n)
3. Count comparisons for sorted array (should be ~n²/2)
4. Print both and show the ratio is ~n/(2 log n)
hints: Sorted array + last-element pivot: pivot is always max, left partition = n-1 elements
Comparison count for sorted: n + (n-1) + ... + 1 = n(n-1)/2 ≈ n²/2
This is a REAL production bug — not a theoretical concern

exercises.title: Fix quicksort worst case with random pivot
exercises.instructions:
Fix the quicksort worst case:
1. Implement quicksort with RANDOM pivot selection
2. Show sorted input no longer triggers O(n²)
3. Compare comparison counts: naive vs random pivot on sorted input of size 1000
4. Both give correct result but random pivot avoids quadratic blowup
hints: Random pivot: swap random element to end, then partition normally
Expected comparisons: O(n log n) for any input with random pivot
Sorted input no longer triggers worst case

exercises.title: HashMap best, worst, average case
exercises.instructions:
Demonstrate HashMap's three cases:
1. Best/Average O(1): good hash function, direct bucket access
2. Worst O(n): many hash collisions (all keys same bucket)
3. Create a BadHashMap where all keys hash to the same bucket
4. Measure lookup time for both at n=10,000
hints: hashCode() returning constant 0: all keys go to same bucket → O(n) linked list scan
Java 8+: bucket converts to Red-Black tree at size 8 → O(log n) worst case
This is why 'HashMap is O(1) average' is more precise than 'HashMap is O(1)'

exercises.title: Binary search: all three cases
exercises.instructions:
Implement binary search with step counter. Demonstrate:
1. Best case O(1): target is exactly at the midpoint
2. Average case O(log n): target at random position
3. Worst case O(log n): target not present (or at boundary)

Run on sorted array of size 1024. Show step counts for each case.
hints: Best case O(1): target happens to be at the first midpoint checked
Worst case O(log n): not present — exhausts the search space
Binary search never degrades past O(log n) — unlike quicksort

exercises.title: Amortized analysis: stack with multipop
exercises.instructions:
A Stack supports push(x) and popAll() (removes all elements).
- push: O(1) per call
- popAll: O(k) where k = current size

Prove that n operations (any mix of push/popAll) have O(n) TOTAL cost → O(1) amortized.

Implement and demonstrate with a sequence of 10 pushes + 1 popAll + 10 more pushes.
hints: Key: each element pushed ONCE, popped at most ONCE
Total pops ≤ total pushes = n
O(n) total cost / n ops = O(1) amortized

exercises.title: Give complete complexity answers for 5 algorithms
exercises.instructions:
For each algorithm, give the COMPLETE interview answer (best, worst, average, space):
1. Bubble sort
2. Insertion sort (note: O(n) best case!)
3. Merge sort
4. Binary search (iterative)
5. HashMap.put()

Print as a formatted table.
hints: Insertion sort: O(n) best case is genuinely different from O(n²) worst
Merge sort: always O(n log n) — no best/worst divergence
HashMap worst: bad hashCode() → all keys in one bucket → O(n)

exercises.title: Identify which case is triggered
exercises.instructions:
For each input to each algorithm, identify: Best / Average / Worst case and explain why.

A: Linear search on `[5,1,3,8,2,7]` looking for `5`
B: Linear search on `[5,1,3,8,2,7]` looking for `9` (not present)
C: Quicksort (last-element pivot) on `[1,2,3,4,5,6,7,8]`
D: Quicksort (last-element pivot) on `[4,2,7,1,9,3,8,5]`
E: Binary search on `[1,3,5,7,9,11]` looking for `7` (exact midpoint at first step)
exercises.expected_solution:
A: Linear search for 5 in [5,1,3,8,2,7]
   → BEST CASE O(1): target is at index 0 — found on first check!

B: Linear search for 9 in [5,1,3,8,2,7]
   → WORST CASE O(n): target not present — scans all 6 elements
   → Also worst case: target at last position

C: Quicksort (last pivot) on [1,2,3,4,5,6,7,8] — SORTED array
   → WORST CASE O(n²):
   Pivot=8 → partition: [1,2,3,4,5,6,7] | [] — completely unbalanced!
   Pivot=7 → partition: [1,2,3,4,5,6] | [] — again unbalanced!
   Recursion depth = n, n comparisons each level → O(n²)
   This triggered Flipkart's 2019 search outage.

D: Quicksort (last pivot) on [4,2,7,1,9,3,8,5] — random order
   → AVERAGE CASE O(n log n):
   Pivot=5 → partition: [4,2,1,3] | [7,9,8] — reasonably balanced
   Random input → expected O(n log n) performance

E: Binary search for 7 in [1,3,5,7,9,11] (size 6)
   → BEST CASE O(1):
   First mid = (0+5)/2 = 2, arr[2] = 5 ≠ 7, not quite...
   Actually: mid=2 → 5, lo=3, mid=4 → 9, hi=3, mid=3 → 7 FOUND
   → AVERAGE/WORST CASE O(log n) in this example (not at exact first mid)
   Best O(1) requires target to be at arr[n/2] — exactly the first midpoint

test_cases.expected:
A: Linear search for 5 in [5,1,3,8,2,7]
   → BEST CASE O(1): target is at index 0 — found on first check!

B: Linear search for 9 in [5,1,3,8,2,7]
   → WORST CASE O(n): target not present — scans all 6 elements
   → Also worst case: target at last position

C: Quicksort (last pivot) on [1,2,3,4,5,6,7,8] — SORTED array
   → WORST CASE O(n²):
   Pivot=8 → partition: [1,2,3,4,5,6,7] | [] — completely unbalanced!
   Pivot=7 → partition: [1,2,3,4,5,6] | [] — again unbalanced!
   Recursion depth = n, n comparisons each level → O(n²)
   This triggered Flipkart's 2019 search outage.

D: Quicksort (last pivot) on [4,2,7,1,9,3,8,5] — random order
   → AVERAGE CASE O(n log n):
   Pivot=5 → partition: [4,2,1,3] | [7,9,8] — reasonably balanced
   Random input → expected O(n log n) performance

E: Binary search for 7 in [1,3,5,7,9,11] (size 6)
   → BEST CASE O(1):
   First mid = (0+5)/2 = 2, arr[2] = 5 ≠ 7, not quite...
   Actually: mid=2 → 5, lo=3, mid=4 → 9, hi=3, mid=3 → 7 FOUND
   → AVERAGE/WORST CASE O(log n) in this example (not at exact first mid)
   Best O(1) requires target to be at arr[n/2] — exactly the first midpoint

hints: Best case: always the most favorable input for that algorithm
Sorted array + last-element pivot: ALWAYS worst case for naive quicksort
Binary search O(1) best: target must be exactly at arr[n/2]

exercises.title: Insertion sort: best case O(n) on nearly-sorted data
exercises.instructions:
Insertion sort has O(n) best case on already-sorted input — the only common sort with this property.

1. Implement insertion sort with swap counter
2. Compare swap counts: sorted vs random input for n=1000
3. Show sorted → O(n) comparisons; random → O(n²) comparisons
4. Explain why TimSort uses insertion sort for small subarrays
hints: Sorted input: inner while loop never executes → O(n) comparisons total
Reversed: maximum swaps — O(n²) inner while executes fully
TimSort exploits this: detects sorted runs and uses insertion sort on them

exercises.title: Amortized analysis: Union-Find (Disjoint Set Union)
exercises.instructions:
Implement Union-Find with path compression and union by rank.
Both operations are amortized O(α(n)) — nearly O(1).

1. Implement find() with path compression
2. Implement union() with rank
3. Show that 10M operations on n=1M elements complete quickly
4. This data structure is used in Kruskal's MST and network connectivity
hints: Path compression: parent[x] = find(parent[x]) — flatten the tree
Union by rank: attach shorter tree under taller
α(n) ≤ 5 for any practical n — this IS effectively O(1)

exercises.title: Complete complexity interview for 3 algorithms
exercises.instructions:
Give the COMPLETE interview answer (all cases + space + trade-off) for:
1. Quicksort
2. Bubble sort
3. Hash table lookup

Format: print exactly what you would SAY in a Flipkart/Zerodha interview.
Then implement a mini-benchmark verifying the stated worst cases.
hints: Interview format: average + worst + when worst occurs + mitigation
HashMap Java 8+: bucket trees prevent O(n) worst case in practice
Always state space complexity alongside time

exercises.title: Quicksort with median-of-three pivot
exercises.instructions:
Implement quicksort with median-of-three pivot (picks median of first, middle, last elements):
1. Show it avoids worst case on sorted AND reverse-sorted input
2. Compare comparison counts: naive vs median-of-three on sorted n=1000
3. Show both give correct result

This is the technique used by many production sort implementations.

expected_stdout_contains: Both produce same sorted output: true

hints: medianOf3: sort arr[lo], arr[mid], arr[hi] by value, median is arr[mid]
For sorted array: median of first, middle, last is the TRUE middle value
Both sorts give same output — we're only changing pivot selection, not correctness

exercises.title: Complete M29 T4 Synthesis — Full Complexity Answer Machine
exercises.instructions:
Build ComplexityAdvisor — given an algorithm name, prints the COMPLETE complexity profile:

Support: 'quicksort', 'mergesort', 'bubblesort', 'binarysearch', 'hashmap', 'linearsearch'

Each answer: best case, worst case, average case, space, stability, when worst is triggered, mitigation.

Also: build a Complexity record and 5 tests verifying the data is correct.
hints: Record: immutable data carrier — perfect for complexity profiles
Merge sort: best==worst==average O(n log n) — always consistent
Bubble sort: O(n) best (with early exit) vs O(n²) worst
